facts.text: A major scale follows the pattern of whole and half steps: W-W-H-W-W-W-H.
The natural minor scale follows the pattern W-H-W-W-H-W-W.
A chromatic scale contains all twelve pitches within an octave, each a half step apart.
The pentatonic scale uses only five notes and is found in music traditions all over the world.
The major pentatonic scale removes the 4th and 7th degrees of the major scale.
The minor pentatonic scale is the basis of most blues and rock guitar solos.
The blues scale adds a 'blue note' (flat 5th) to the minor pentatonic scale, giving it six notes.
The whole tone scale has six notes, each separated by a whole step, creating a dreamy, ambiguous sound.
There are seven modes of the major scale: Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, and Locrian.
The Dorian mode is a minor scale with a raised 6th degree, commonly used in jazz and funk.
The Mixolydian mode is a major scale with a lowered 7th, widely used in rock, blues, and folk.
The Phrygian mode has a distinctive flamenco-like sound due to its lowered 2nd degree.
The Lydian mode raises the 4th degree of the major scale, giving it a bright, floating quality.
The Locrian mode is the only mode with a diminished tonic triad, making it the least stable.
The Aeolian mode is identical to the natural minor scale.
The harmonic minor scale raises the 7th degree of the natural minor, creating a leading tone.
The melodic minor scale raises both the 6th and 7th degrees when ascending in classical practice.
In jazz, the melodic minor scale is played the same way ascending and descending.
A triad is a three-note chord built by stacking two thirds.
The four types of triads are major, minor, augmented, and diminished.
A major triad consists of a root, major third, and perfect fifth.
A minor triad consists of a root, minor third, and perfect fifth.
An augmented triad has a root, major third, and augmented fifth.
A diminished triad has a root, minor third, and diminished fifth.
A dominant 7th chord adds a minor 7th interval on top of a major triad.
A major 7th chord adds a major 7th interval on top of a major triad, common in jazz and R&B.
A minor 7th chord adds a minor 7th to a minor triad.
A half-diminished 7th chord is a diminished triad with a minor 7th, also called minor 7 flat 5.
Extended chords like 9ths, 11ths, and 13ths add color beyond basic 7th chords.
A dominant 9th chord adds the 9th to a dominant 7th chord.
An 11th chord includes the root, 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th, and 11th, though some notes are often omitted.
A 13th chord theoretically contains all seven notes of the scale stacked in thirds.
In practice, 13th chords omit certain notes — commonly the 11th and sometimes the 5th.
A suspended chord replaces the 3rd with either a 2nd (sus2) or 4th (sus4).
Sus chords create tension that wants to resolve back to a major or minor chord.
Power chords contain only the root and fifth, making them neither major nor minor.
An interval is the distance in pitch between two notes.
A half step (semitone) is the smallest interval in Western music.
A whole step equals two half steps, such as from C to D.
A perfect fifth spans seven half steps and is one of the most consonant intervals.
An octave is the interval between one pitch and the next pitch with double or half its frequency.
A major third spans four half steps, while a minor third spans three.
Intervals can be melodic (notes in sequence) or harmonic (notes played simultaneously).
Inverting an interval means moving the lower note up an octave; a 5th inverts to a 4th.
Compound intervals are larger than an octave, like a 9th (octave plus a 2nd).
The perfect intervals are the unison, fourth, fifth, and octave.
A key signature tells the performer which notes are sharp or flat throughout a piece.
The key of C major has no sharps or flats.
Sharp key signatures add sharps in this order: F, C, G, D, A, E, B.
Flat key signatures add flats in this order: B, E, A, D, G, C, F — the reverse of sharps.
Every major key has a relative minor that shares the same key signature.
The relative minor is found a minor third below the major key's tonic.
Enharmonic keys sound identical but are spelled differently, like F# major and Gb major.
4/4 time is the most common time signature in Western music, also called 'common time.'
3/4 time is the time signature of waltzes, giving a strong ONE-two-three feel.
6/8 time is a compound meter that groups beats in two sets of three eighth notes.
Cut time (2/2) is often used in marches and fast orchestral pieces.
7/8 time is common in Balkan folk music and progressive rock.
Simple meters divide beats into two equal parts; compound meters divide beats into three.
Syncopation places emphasis on normally weak beats or off-beats, creating rhythmic surprise.
A polyrhythm occurs when two or more conflicting rhythmic patterns are played simultaneously.
The 3-against-2 polyrhythm is one of the most common, found in African drumming and jazz.
Swing rhythm lengthens the first of each pair of eighth notes, giving jazz its characteristic groove.
A hemiola is a rhythmic device where two bars of triple meter sound like three bars of duple.
A dotted note extends its duration by half its original value.
A tie connects two notes of the same pitch, combining them into a single sustained note.
Harmony is the simultaneous combination of notes to produce chords and chord progressions.
Consonant harmonies sound stable; dissonant harmonies create tension that seeks resolution.
Tertian harmony is built from stacking thirds and is the basis of most Western music.
Quartal harmony stacks fourths instead of thirds, creating an open, modern sound used in jazz.
Voice leading is the art of smoothly connecting one chord to the next, minimizing large jumps.
In four-part harmony, the four voices are soprano, alto, tenor, and bass.
Chord inversions rearrange the notes so the root is not the lowest note.
A first inversion chord has the third as the lowest note; second inversion has the fifth.
Roman numeral analysis labels chords by their scale degree: I, ii, iii, IV, V, vi, vii°.
Upper-case Roman numerals indicate major chords; lower-case indicate minor chords.
A melody is a sequence of single notes perceived as a unified musical idea.
Melodies typically move by step (conjunct motion) with occasional leaps (disjunct motion).
Antecedent and consequent phrases form a 'question and answer' structure in melody.
A sequence is a melodic pattern repeated at a higher or lower pitch level.
Motifs are short melodic fragments that serve as building blocks for larger compositions.
Contour describes the overall shape of a melody — whether it rises, falls, or stays level.
Counterpoint is the art of combining two or more independent melodic lines simultaneously.
Species counterpoint is a teaching method that introduces contrapuntal rules in five stages.
A canon is a contrapuntal form where a melody is imitated by one or more voices at fixed intervals.
A fugue is a complex contrapuntal composition built on a subject introduced across multiple voices.
Contrary motion occurs when two voices move in opposite directions.
Parallel fifths and octaves are generally avoided in classical counterpoint and harmony.
Verse-chorus form is the dominant structure in pop and rock music.
The 12-bar blues is a chord progression using I, IV, and V chords over 12 measures.
Sonata form has three main sections: exposition, development, and recapitulation.
Rondo form features a recurring main theme alternating with contrasting episodes (ABACA).
Binary form (AB) has two contrasting sections and was common in Baroque dance suites.
Ternary form (ABA) presents a theme, a contrasting section, and a return of the original theme.
Through-composed form has no repeating sections — each part uses new music.
Strophic form repeats the same music for each verse, like in many hymns and folk songs.
A coda is a concluding passage added to the end of a piece to bring it to a satisfying close.
AABA form (32-bar form) was standard for Tin Pan Alley songs and early jazz standards.
Theme and variations form presents a melody and then alters it in successive sections.
The treble clef is also called the G clef because it wraps around the line representing G.
The bass clef is also known as the F clef because its two dots surround the line for F.
The alto clef (C clef) is primarily used by violas and centers middle C on the third line.
The sharp symbol raises a note by one half step; the flat symbol lowers it by one half step.
A natural sign cancels a preceding sharp or flat within a measure.
Ledger lines extend the staff to notate pitches above or below the five standard lines.
Equal temperament divides the octave into 12 equal half steps so every key sounds equally in tune.
Just intonation tunes intervals to simple whole-number frequency ratios, like 3:2 for a perfect fifth.
Pythagorean tuning is based entirely on stacking perfect fifths (3:2 ratio).
Meantone temperament was popular during the Renaissance, producing sweeter thirds at the expense of some fifths.
A440 means the A above middle C vibrates at 440 Hz — the modern standard tuning pitch.
Ear training is the practice of learning to identify pitches, intervals, chords, and rhythms by ear.
Relative pitch is the ability to identify intervals and notes in relation to a reference pitch.
Solfège (do, re, mi, fa, sol, la, ti) is widely used for ear training and sight-singing.
Fixed-do solfège always assigns 'do' to C; movable-do assigns 'do' to the tonic of any key.
Transposition means moving a piece of music up or down in pitch by a consistent interval.
When transposing for a Bb instrument, you write music a whole step higher than concert pitch.
Modulation is the process of changing from one key to another within a piece.
A pivot chord modulation uses a chord common to both keys to smoothly transition.
Direct modulation changes key without any transitional chords.
Modulating to a closely related key sounds smooth; modulating to a distant key is dramatic.
A cadence is a harmonic formula that ends a phrase, section, or piece of music.
An authentic cadence (V-I) is the strongest and most final-sounding cadence.
A perfect authentic cadence has both chords in root position with the tonic in the soprano.
A half cadence ends on the V chord and sounds incomplete, like a musical comma.
A deceptive cadence replaces the expected I chord with vi, surprising the listener.
The circle of fifths is a diagram showing the relationships among all 12 chromatic pitches.
Moving clockwise around the circle of fifths, each key adds one sharp.
Moving counterclockwise, each key adds one flat.
Dynamics in music range from pianissimo (very soft) to fortissimo (very loud).
Crescendo means gradually getting louder; diminuendo means gradually getting softer.
Sforzando (sfz) indicates a sudden, strong accent on a single note or chord.
Mezzo-forte (mf) means moderately loud and is roughly the default dynamic level.
Tempo is the speed at which music is performed, measured in beats per minute (BPM).
Allegro indicates a fast, lively tempo, usually around 120-156 BPM.
Adagio means a slow, relaxed tempo, typically around 66-76 BPM.
Presto is a very fast tempo, around 168-200 BPM.
Rubato is the practice of slightly speeding up and slowing down for expressive effect.
Accelerando means gradually increasing the tempo; ritardando means gradually slowing down.
Staccato means notes are played short and detached, indicated by a dot above or below the note.
Legato means notes are played smoothly and connected, often indicated by a slur.
Tenuto means to hold a note for its full value, sometimes with slight emphasis.
A fermata tells the performer to hold a note longer than its written value.
A trill rapidly alternates between a note and the note above it.
A glissando is a slide between two notes, passing through all pitches in between.
Pizzicato instructs a string player to pluck the strings instead of bowing.
Orchestration is the art of assigning musical parts to different instruments.
Doubling means giving the same melody to two or more instruments, thickening the sound.
Brass instruments can produce powerful, projecting tones suitable for climactic passages.
Woodwinds offer a wide variety of timbres, from the warmth of a clarinet to the brightness of a flute.
Most pop songs use only three to four chords from the same key.
Blues music is typically based on dominant 7th chords, which give it its characteristic sound.
The ii-V-I progression is the most fundamental chord movement in jazz harmony.
Jazz musicians often add extensions (9ths, 11ths, 13ths) and alterations to basic chords.
Modal interchange (borrowed chords) uses chords from a parallel key to add color.
The overtone series is a naturally occurring sequence of frequencies above any fundamental pitch.
The first overtone of any note is exactly one octave higher.
A chord progression establishes tonality by creating tension and resolution.
The V chord (dominant) creates the strongest pull toward the tonic.
Secondary dominants temporarily tonicize a non-tonic chord.
A pedal point is a sustained bass note over which harmonies change above it.
An ostinato is a repeating musical pattern that persists throughout a passage.
A passing tone is a non-chord tone that moves stepwise between two chord tones.
A neighbor tone moves one step away from a chord tone and returns.
An appoggiatura is approached by leap and resolved by step, creating expressive dissonance.
A suspension holds a note from a previous chord into the next, then resolves downward.
Figured bass was a Baroque-era notation system using numbers below bass notes to indicate chords.
An anacrusis (pickup) is one or more notes before the first full measure of music.
The Neapolitan chord is a major triad built on the lowered second degree, usually in first inversion.
Augmented sixth chords (Italian, French, German) are chromatic chords that resolve to the dominant.
Chromaticism introduces notes outside the prevailing key for color or modulation.
Atonality is music that lacks a tonal center, pioneered by Schoenberg.
Serialism extends the twelve-tone method to organize rhythm, dynamics, and other elements.
Polytonality uses two or more keys simultaneously.
A tone row in twelve-tone music arranges all 12 chromatic notes in a specific order.
A tone row can be used in four forms: original, retrograde, inversion, and retrograde inversion.
Minimalism uses repetitive patterns with gradual changes, as in works by Steve Reich and Philip Glass.
Phase shifting has two identical patterns gradually going out of sync.
The harmonic rhythm of a piece refers to how frequently its chords change.
Slow harmonic rhythm (chords changing every few bars) is common in rock and folk.
Fast harmonic rhythm (chords changing every beat) is common in bebop jazz.
The tonic (first degree) is the 'home base' of a key, providing rest and stability.
The dominant (fifth degree) creates the strongest pull toward the tonic.
The leading tone is one half step below the tonic, pulling strongly upward.
The supertonic (second degree) often serves a predominant function as part of a ii chord.
A ground bass is a repeating bass line over which upper parts are freely varied.
Chord voicing refers to how the notes of a chord are distributed across registers.
Close voicing keeps chord notes within one octave; open voicing spreads them wider.
Drop-2 voicings move the second-highest note down an octave and are common on guitar.
Rootless voicings omit the root, relying on the bass to provide it, common in jazz piano.
The bebop dominant scale adds a natural 7th to the Mixolydian scale.
The altered scale (7th mode of melodic minor) is used over altered dominant chords in jazz.
A vamp is a repeating chord progression or riff, often used as an intro or outro.
Call and response is a structure where a phrase by one part is answered by another.
A grace note is a small, quick ornamental note played just before a main note.
A mordent rapidly alternates a note with the note directly above or below it.
A cadenza is a virtuoso passage near the end of a concerto movement.
A reharmonization replaces the original chords of a melody with different chords.
Phrase rhythm studies how melodic phrases interact with the underlying meter.
Hypermeter groups measures into larger metric units, like four-bar phrases as a single unit.
Metric modulation changes tempo by redefining which note value gets the beat.
Additive rhythm builds patterns by combining groups of 2s and 3s.
Graphic notation uses visual symbols rather than traditional notes.
Tablature indicates fingering positions rather than pitches, common for guitar.
The concept of consonance and dissonance has shifted greatly through music history.
A drone is a sustained bass note or chord held throughout a passage.
Chord substitution replaces a chord with a harmonically related one for variety.
A passing chord is inserted between two chords for smooth voice leading.
Chromatic mediants are chords a third apart that share one note, creating colorful shifts.
Cross-rhythm occurs when the rhythmic pattern of one part conflicts with another.
Polymeters occur when two different time signatures are played simultaneously.
Cents measure intervals — there are 100 cents per half step and 1200 per octave.
Just intonation produces purer thirds than equal temperament, but limits key changes.
The great staff (grand staff) combines treble and bass clefs for piano notation.
Middle C sits on a ledger line between the treble and bass staves.
Enharmonic equivalence means two notes sound the same but are spelled differently (C# and Db).
The French horn is in F — its written C sounds as concert F.
An augmented interval is one half step wider than a perfect or major interval.
A diminished interval is one half step narrower than a perfect or minor interval.
An added-tone chord includes a note outside the normal tertian stack, like Cadd9.
Slash chords indicate a bass note different from the root, like C/E.
Arpeggiation plays the notes of a chord one at a time rather than simultaneously.
Functional harmony categorizes every chord as tonic, predominant, or dominant.
Non-functional harmony uses chords for color rather than resolution, common in film scoring.
Impressionist harmony often uses whole-tone and pentatonic scales to avoid traditional resolution.
The octatonic (diminished) scale alternates whole and half steps.
Chord-scale theory matches each chord with a specific scale for improvisation.
Lead sheets show only the melody and chord symbols, leaving accompaniment to the performer.
A turnaround is a short progression at the end of a section that leads back to the beginning.
Guide tones (the 3rd and 7th of each chord) most clearly define a chord's quality.
Sight-reading is performing music at first sight without prior practice.
The Kodály method uses singing-based instruction with hand signs for musical literacy.
A minor second (one half step) is the most dissonant interval in tonal music.
The cadential 6/4 chord resolves to V, creating a strong cadence.
Part writing rules include avoiding parallel octaves, fifths, and voice crossing.
A musical phrase typically spans 4 or 8 measures in Western music.
Phrase extension delays the expected cadence for dramatic effect.
A pitch class refers to all octave duplications of a single note.
Figured bass numbers like 6/3 indicate a first inversion triad.
Da capo (D.C.) means go back to the beginning of the piece.
Tempo giusto means playing at the speed implied by the music's character.
Largo means broadly, typically around 40-60 BPM.
Moderato indicates a moderate speed, typically around 108-120 BPM.
Vivace means lively and fast, generally between Allegro and Presto.
Timbre, or tone color, is what makes a violin sound different from a flute on the same note.
Attack, decay, sustain, and release (ADSR) describe the amplitude envelope of a sound.
Invertible counterpoint means two melodic lines can switch positions — either can be on top.
A stretto in a fugue brings in subject entries earlier than expected, increasing intensity.
The exposition of a fugue presents the subject in all voices at least once.
Spectral music analyzes overtone content and uses that data to create harmony.
Formants are resonant frequencies in the vocal tract that define vowel sounds.
The Ionian mode is identical to the major scale.
A retardation is like a suspension but resolves upward instead of downward.
An escape tone is a non-chord tone approached by step and left by leap.
Oblique motion occurs when one voice holds a note while the other moves.
A bridge in popular music provides contrast before the final chorus.
The I-vi-ii-V turnaround is one of the most common in jazz.
Integer notation represents pitch classes as numbers 0-11, where C=0, C#=1, D=2, and so on.
Tuplets divide a beat into an irregular number of parts, such as triplets dividing one beat into three.
V/V (five of five) is the most common secondary dominant.
Common-tone modulation holds one or more shared notes between the old and new keys.
Fortepiano (fp) means to play a note loudly and immediately drop to soft.
Tremolo is the rapid repetition of a single note or alternation between two notes.
A marcato is a stronger accent than a regular accent, indicated by a caret symbol.
Parallel harmony moves all voices in the same direction by the same interval.
Double sharps and double flats raise or lower notes by a whole step.
The 'Andalusian cadence' (i-bVII-bVI-V) is a descending progression heard in flamenco and pop.
Upper structure triads place a simple triad over a bass tritone to create altered dominant sounds.
Slash notation in jazz charts shows rhythm slashes for free comping.
Playing 'outside' in jazz means using notes that clash with the harmony for tension.
Approach tones in jazz are chromatic or diatonic notes that lead into a chord tone.
The CAGED system organizes the guitar fretboard into five interlocking chord shapes.
A tritone substitution replaces a dominant chord with another a tritone away.
The tritone was historically called 'diabolus in musica' — the devil in music.
A diminished 7th chord stacks three minor thirds, dividing the octave into four equal parts.
Power chords are a staple of punk and rock guitar because of their raw, overdriven sound.
There are 15 major key signatures, but only 12 unique-sounding ones because three pairs are enharmonic.
5/4 time is an odd meter famously used in Dave Brubeck's 'Take Five.'
Syncopation is a defining feature of jazz, funk, reggae, and Latin music.
The I-IV-V-I progression is the foundation of countless songs in Western music.
The vi-IV-I-V progression is one of the most popular chord progressions in modern pop.
Beethoven's Fifth Symphony is built almost entirely from a four-note motif.
'Row, Row, Row Your Boat' is a well-known example of a simple canon (a round).
Bach's 'Well-Tempered Clavier' contains 48 preludes and fugues, two in every major and minor key.
The 12-bar blues form is the backbone of blues, early rock and roll, and many jazz standards.
Modern Western notation evolved from neumes used in medieval Gregorian chant.
Guido of Arezzo introduced solmization syllables (ut, re, mi, fa, sol, la) for sight-singing.
Time signatures were not standardized until the 14th century.
Before equal temperament, some keys sounded more in tune than others, limiting key choices.
The Pythagorean comma is the small discrepancy because 12 perfect fifths don't equal 7 octaves.
In the Baroque era, tuning pitch varied widely and could be as low as A=392 Hz.
Only about 1 in 10,000 people are estimated to have true perfect pitch.
The plagal cadence (IV-I) is sometimes called the 'Amen cadence' because of its use in hymns.
The Picardy third ends a minor-key piece on a major tonic chord.
Adjacent keys on the circle of fifths share six of seven notes.
The instrument called a 'piano' was originally named 'pianoforte' because it could play soft and loud.
Beethoven was one of the first major composers to include metronome markings in his scores.
Rimsky-Korsakov's orchestration textbook, published posthumously in 1912, remains widely used.
Strings are considered the backbone of the orchestra because of their blend and dynamic range.
Pythagoras is credited with discovering that musical intervals can be expressed as ratios.
Boethius wrote 'De Institutione Musica' around 500 AD, shaping theory education for centuries.
Hugo Riemann developed functional harmony, categorizing chords as tonic, dominant, or subdominant.
Heinrich Schenker proposed that all tonal music reduces to a fundamental background structure.
Allen Forte created set theory for music, a mathematical approach to analyzing atonal works.
The Nashville Number System uses numbers instead of chord names, making transposition easy.
The I-V-vi-IV progression is so common in pop it has been called the 'four-chord song.'
The bVII chord (like Bb in C major) is borrowed from the parallel minor and is common in rock.
Helmholtz's 'On the Sensations of Tone' (1863) was a pioneering work in musical acoustics.
The Alberti bass is a broken chord pattern common in Classical piano, named after Domenico Alberti.
Basso continuo was the Baroque practice of improvising harmony from figured bass.
The 'Tristan chord' from Wagner's opera is one of the most analyzed chords in music history.
Microtonality uses intervals smaller than a half step, common in some Middle Eastern traditions.
Henry Cowell pioneered tone clusters, playing the piano with fists and forearms.
The flat-VII to I progression (like Bb to C) is a hallmark of classic rock.
Planing moves a chord shape in parallel motion, a technique famously used by Debussy.
Coltrane changes use major-third key movements instead of the usual fifth-based motion.
Call and response has roots in African musical traditions and is central to blues and gospel.
Rachmaninoff could reportedly span a 13th on the piano, influencing his chord voicings.
Ornaments were extensively used in Baroque music and were often improvised.
Neo-Riemannian theory analyzes chord relationships through geometric transformations.
Negative harmony, popularized by Jacob Collier, mirrors intervals to create new progressions.
Bartók often used Fibonacci numbers and the golden ratio for structural proportions.
Indian raga theory classifies modes by ascending/descending patterns and characteristic phrases.
Maqam is the system of melodic modes in Arabic and Turkish music with microtonal intervals.
Gamelan music from Indonesia uses non-Western tuning systems called slendro and pelog.
A fake book is a collection of jazz lead sheets, originally circulated illegally.
The Real Book was compiled in the 1970s at Berklee and became the most used fake book.
The 'rhythm changes' progression is based on Gershwin's 'I Got Rhythm.'
Charlie Parker's 'Ornithology' uses the chord changes from 'How High the Moon.'
Pentatonic scales work over many chord types because they avoid dissonant half steps.
In medieval music, thirds were considered dissonant — only octaves, fifths, and fourths were consonant.
Transposing instruments exist because early brass instruments could only play in certain keys.
Barbershop quartet singing naturally gravitates toward just intonation.
Film composers like John Williams frequently use chromatic mediant relations for drama.
A leitmotif is a recurring theme tied to a character or idea, perfected by Wagner.
The Lydian Chromatic Concept considers the Lydian mode as the most fundamental scale.
Audiation — hearing music internally while reading notation — was coined by Edwin Gordon.
Quartal voicings became a signature of McCoy Tyner's jazz piano style.
Pachelbel's Canon in D uses a repeating eight-chord progression recognized worldwide.
La Folia is one of the oldest European chord progressions, dating to the late 15th century.
Bach harmonized over 370 chorales, which remain the gold standard for part-writing study.
Tchaikovsky used pppppp (six p's) in his Sixth Symphony — one of the softest markings ever.
Verdi advocated for A=432 Hz, and some musicians still prefer this lower tuning.
Guido of Arezzo developed the basis of modern staff notation in the 11th century.
Bach's 'Well-Tempered Clavier' Book I was composed in 1722, showcasing all 24 keys.
Mozart was born on January 27, 1756, and composed masterfully in nearly every genre.
Palestrina was born on February 2, 1525; his works are still studied as counterpoint models.
Well temperament was in use in Bach's era, making all keys playable yet each unique.
Schoenberg's 'Harmonielehre' was published in 1911, a foundational modern harmony textbook.
Hector Berlioz wrote one of the first major treatises on orchestration in 1844.
Arnold Schoenberg developed the twelve-tone technique around 1921, treating all 12 notes as equal.
J.S. Bach was born on March 31, 1685, widely regarded as the greatest master of counterpoint.
Giuseppe Tartini discovered combination tones in 1714, calling them the 'third sound.'
The first printed music book using movable type was published in 1501 by Ottaviano Petrucci.
Concert pitch A was standardized at 440 Hz at a 1939 international conference.
Stravinsky's 'Rite of Spring' premiere on May 29, 1913, caused a riot due to its radical rhythms.
The 'Ars Nova' treatise by Philippe de Vitry (c. 1322) introduced rhythmic innovations to notation.
Wagner's 'Tristan und Isolde' (1865) pushed chromaticism to extremes, blurring tonality.
Stravinsky was born on June 17, 1882; his works revolutionized rhythm in 20th-century music.
Johann Joseph Fux's 'Gradus ad Parnassum' (1725) codified the rules of species counterpoint.
The Tonnetz diagram for mapping pitch relationships was formalized in the 19th century.
MIDI was introduced in August 1983, allowing electronic instruments to communicate digitally.
Claude Debussy was born on August 22, 1862, and pioneered impressionist harmony.
John Cage premiered '4′33″' on August 29, 1952, a piece consisting entirely of silence.
George Russell's 'Lydian Chromatic Concept' (1953) was a landmark in jazz theory.
Schoenberg was born on September 13, 1874, and revolutionized music with the twelve-tone method.
Jean-Philippe Rameau published his 'Treatise on Harmony' in 1722, founding modern harmonic theory.
David Lewin published 'Generalized Musical Intervals and Transformations' in 1987.
Wagner published 'Opera and Drama' in 1851, outlining his Gesamtkunstwerk concept.
The Werckmeister temperaments, published in 1691, were among the most used well temperaments.
Andreas Werckmeister was born on November 30, 1645; his tunings helped pave the way for Bach.
The metronome was patented by Johann Maelzel in 1815.
Olivier Messiaen was born on December 10, 1908; he developed modes of limited transposition.
Beethoven was born on December 17, 1770, bridging the Classical and Romantic eras.
The Mannheim School, active in the mid-1700s, pioneered orchestral crescendos and dynamic contrasts.
Zarlino published 'Le Istitutioni Harmoniche' in 1558, establishing the theoretical basis for triadic harmony.
Musica Enchiriadis, written around 895 AD, contains the earliest known examples of written polyphony.
The Conservatoire de Paris was founded on August 3, 1795, and became a major center for theory education.
A contrafact in jazz is a new melody composed over the chord changes of an existing standard.
A score written in concert pitch shows all instruments as they actually sound.
The diminished scale starting with a half step is often used over diminished 7th chords in jazz.
